subtitle: Four Witnesses to the City of Victory

panels.shot: epic wide shot from a rocky hill above Vijayanagara
panels.action: The Four Witnesses of Vijayanagara stand on a granite outcrop, each in distinct foreign attire, gazing over a sprawling city of walls, temples and markets nestled among boulders and the Tungabhadra river.

dialogue.text: From Italy, Persia and Portugal, four travelers reached Vijayanagara between 1420 and 1537, leaving words that still glow with the dust and splendor of the City of Victory.

i18n.hi: इटली, फ़ारस और पुर्तगाल से चार यात्री 1420 से 1537 के बीच विजयनगर पहुँचे, धूल और शान से चमकती विजय नगरी की बातें छोड़ गए।

panels.shot: medium shot montage of four faces separated by visual panels
panels.action: Split composition shows Nicolo de' Conti with Italian robes and a travel staff, Abdur Razzaq in Persian court dress, Domingo Paes in simple Portuguese trader garb, and Fernao Nunes as a scribe, each framed by different landscapes.

dialogue.text: Nicolo de’ Conti around 1420, Abdur Razzaq in 1443, Domingo Paes in 1520, and Fernao Nunes in the 1530s—each saw Vijayanagara in a different reign, yet spoke of the same vast city.

i18n.hi: लगभग 1420 में निकोलो दे कॉन्ती, 1443 में अब्दुर रज़्ज़ाक, 1520 में दोमिंगो पायस और 1530 के दशक में फ़ेरनाओ नूनेश — चारों ने अलग राज में वही विशाल विजयनगर देखा।

panels.shot: sweeping bird’s-eye over Indian Ocean routes
panels.action: Map-like scene: ships on the Indian Ocean, caravan lines across Persia and Deccan, with faint routes traced from Venice, Herat and Lisbon converging toward the interior where Vijayanagara’s hills rise.

dialogue.text: In the fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, ocean winds and caravan roads carried merchants, ambassadors and exiles toward a rising power in the south—Vijayanagara, the City of Victory.

i18n.hi: पंद्रहवीं और शुरुआती सोलहवीं सदी में समंदर की हवाएँ और क़ाफ़िलों के रास्ते व्यापारियों, वज़ीरों और निर्वासितों को दक्षिण की उभरती ताकत — विजय नगरी, विजयनगर — तक ले आए।

panels.shot: slow push-in on Nicolo de’ Conti walking a dusty road
panels.action: Nicolo de’ Conti strides along a dusty inland road, clutching a small bundle of manuscripts, passing palm groves and rocky outcrops that hint at the terrain around Hampi.

dialogue.text: Nicolo de’ Conti, an Italian who wandered twenty-five years in the East, reached the great southern city around 1420, long before Europeans spoke of Calicut and Goa as they later would.

i18n.hi: निकोलो दे कॉन्ती, जो पचीस बरस पूरब में भटका, लगभग 1420 में इस बड़े दक्षिणी शहर पहुँचा, बहुत पहले जब यूरोप वाले कलिकट और गोवा की बातें भी नहीं करते थे।

panels.shot: wide shot of early Vijayanagara from a hill at sunrise
panels.action: Conti stands atop a hill at dawn, watching a fortified city spread below—rows of houses, temple spires, encircling walls and water tanks glowing in the soft light.

dialogue.text: Conti saw a large, rich city with many temples and strong walls, ruled by a Hindu king whose power stretched far across the south—an early glimpse of Vijayanagara’s growing might.

i18n.hi: कॉन्ती ने मंदिरों और मज़बूत किलों वाला बड़ा, दौलतमंद शहर देखा, जहाँ एक हिंदू राजा का दम पूरे दक्षिण तक फैला था — विजयनगर की ताकत की शुरुआती झलक।

panels.shot: interior close medium of Conti speaking to Poggio Bracciolini
panels.action: Later in Italy, Nicolo de’ Conti sits in a study lined with Latin books, describing eastern cities to Poggio Bracciolini, who writes diligently at a heavy wooden table.

dialogue.text: Years later, his memories flowed into Latin through Poggio Bracciolini’s pen, in De Varietate Fortunae, turning his wandering into one of Europe’s first descriptions of Vijayanagara.

i18n.hi: सालों बाद उसकी यादें पोज्जियो ब्राच्चोलिनी की कलम से लैटिन में बहीं, ‘डे वारिएटाते फ़ॉर्च्यूनाए’ में, उसकी यात्राओं को यूरोप की पहली विजयनगर कहानी बना दिया।

panels.shot: majestic low-angle of Abdur Razzaq at a bustling Persian port
panels.action: Abdur Razzaq stands poised at a Persian port, robes catching the sea breeze, a ship to India behind him and officials bearing an inscribed letter from the Timurid ruler.

dialogue.text: In 1443, Abdur Razzaq, envoy of the Timurid court, sailed from Persia to India, bearing letters and expectations, charged to measure the strength of this distant infidel king.

i18n.hi: 1443 में तैमूरी दरबार का वज़ीर अब्दुर रज़्ज़ाक फ़ारस से ख़त और उम्मीदें लेकर हिंदुस्तान चला, इस दूर के काफ़िर बादशाह की ताकत तौलने भेजा गया।

panels.shot: wide shot of Vijayanagara’s royal street as Razzaq arrives
panels.action: Abdur Razzaq rides into Vijayanagara along a broad royal street lined with pillared mandapas, shops and crowds, flanked by armed guards and curious onlookers.

dialogue.text: Razzaq entered Deva Raya II’s court and was struck by its order: long streets, bazaars, and fortified enclosures, a capital vast enough to rival cities he knew in Persia.

i18n.hi: रज़्ज़ाक देवराय द्वितीय के दरबार में दाखिल हुआ और उसकी रौनक से दंग रह गया — लंबी सड़कें, बाज़ार, किलेबंद मुहल्ले, फ़ारसी शहरों टक्कर की राजधानी।

panels.shot: medium interior of Razzaq in audience hall
panels.action: Abdur Razzaq stands in a grand hall before Deva Raya II seated on a throne; behind, musicians and court officials, with open courtyards glimpsed through carved arches.

dialogue.text: He wrote of Deva Raya’s wealth and courtesies, of elephants, cavalry and infantry in numbers almost beyond belief, praising the king’s discipline and the scale of his armies.

i18n.hi: वो देवराय की दौलत और अदब, हाथियों, घुड़सवारों और पैदल फौज के असीम जत्थों को लिखता रहा, राजा की सख़्त अनुशासन और विशाल सेना की तारीफ़ करते हुए।

panels.shot: close-up of Razzaq writing by lamplight
panels.action: Razzaq bends over a low desk, tracing elegant Persian script by lamplight, his words flowing into Matla-us-Sadain as he shapes his impressions in courtly, ornate phrases.

dialogue.text: In Matla-us-Sadain he cast Vijayanagara in Persianate mirrors—mixing fact with literary flourish, counting troops in tens of thousands, arranging the city in the familiar language of empire.

i18n.hi: ‘मतला-उस-सदै़न’ में उसने विजयनगर को फ़ारसी आईनों में दिखाया — हक़ीक़त को सजावटी लफ़्ज़ों से मिलाकर, फ़ौज को लाखों में गिनकर, शहर को सल्तनत की भाषा में सजाया।

panels.shot: dynamic wide of Krishnadevaraya’s Vijayanagara with markets and temple towers
panels.action: Domingo Paes, among other foreigners, enters a later Vijayanagara under Krishnadevaraya, its avenues crowded, bazaars bursting with textiles and spices, temple towers rising above tiled roofs.

dialogue.text: Nearly eighty years later, Domingo Paes reached Krishnadevaraya’s court around 1520, finding the city at its zenith—rich markets, soaring gopuras, and a ruler famed for war and poetry.

i18n.hi: करीब अस्सी साल बाद दोमिंगो पायस 1520 के आसपास कृष्णदेवराय के दरबार पहुँचा, शहर को उसके चरम पर पाया — दौलतमंद बाज़ार, उड़ती गोपुरम, जंग और कविता में मशहूर राजा।

panels.shot: low-angle wide of the royal stables and horse traders
panels.action: Paes walks through immense royal stables, rows of fine horses from Arabia and Persia, while Portuguese and Muslim traders haggle with Vijayanagara officials under tiled roofs.

dialogue.text: Paes saw what mattered to him most: horses. He described endless stables and brisk trade, for he had his own interests in the business that tied Goa’s ports to this inland power.

i18n.hi: पायस की नज़र सबसे ज़्यादा घोड़ों पर थी — उसे अंतहीन अस्तबल और तेज़ रफ़्तार सौदा दिखाई दिया, क्यूंकि वही धंधा गोवा के बंदरगाहों को इस अंदरूनी ताकत से जोड़े रखता था।

panels.shot: epic wide of Mahanavami festival platform
panels.action: A vast stone platform is filled with processions: caparisoned elephants, lines of soldiers, dancers and priests, as the king watches from an elevated pavilion, with Paes standing at the edge of the crowd.

dialogue.text: He marveled at the Mahanavami festival, where Krishnadevaraya reviewed armies on towering platforms, turned the city into a theatre of power, and made ritual and military spectacle one.

i18n.hi: वो महानवमी उत्सव देखकर हैरान रह गया — कृष्णदेवराय ऊँचे मंचों से फौजों का जायज़ा लेते, शहर को ताकत के रंगमंच में बदलते, रस्म और जंगी तमाशा एक कर देते थे।

panels.shot: close-up of Paes watching the king conduct affairs
panels.action: Paes stands quietly near a pillared hall, observing Krishnadevaraya consulting ministers, scribes taking notes, and petitioners waiting in disciplined lines.

dialogue.text: In his report, Paes praised the king’s justice and diligence, describing daily audiences and careful listening, a monarch who balanced war with administration and ritual with routine.

i18n.hi: अपनी रिपोर्ट में पायस ने राजा की इंसाफ़पसंदी और मेहनत की तारीफ़ की — रोज़ाना दिवान, ध्यान से सुनना, एक ऐसा शासक जो युद्ध और हुकूमत, रस्म और रोज़मर्रा में संतुलन रखता था।

panels.shot: medium shot of Fernao Nunes at a desk with Paes’s manuscript
panels.action: Fernao Nunes sits in a simple room, Paes’s notes and other papers spread before him, as he writes a more elaborate Portuguese chronicle years later, quill moving swiftly.

dialogue.text: In the 1530s, Fernao Nunes, a later Portuguese observer, gathered Paes’s account and his own impressions, adding detail and numbers, shaping a narrative of Vijayanagara’s power and decline.

i18n.hi: 1530 के दशक में बाद के पुर्तगाली नज़रबान फ़ेरनाओ नूनेश ने पायस की बातें और अपनी यादें जोड़कर, नए हिसाब-किताब लगाए, विजयनगर की ताकत और पतन की कहानी को तराशा।

panels.shot: wide cosmopolitan street scene with mixed communities
panels.action: All four witnesses are ghosted against a vibrant street of Vijayanagara: Muslim traders, Hindu priests, artisans, foreign merchants and soldiers mingling amid shops and shrines.

dialogue.text: Across their different voices runs a common thread: a city of walls and bazaars, temples and armies, where Hindus, Muslims, and foreigners lived and traded in a dense, cosmopolitan world.

i18n.hi: उनके अलग-अलग सुरों में एक ही डोर चलती है — दीवारों और बाज़ारों, मंदिरों और फौजों का शहर, जहाँ हिंदू, मुसलमान और परदेसी घुलमिल कर तंग, रौनकदार दुनिया में रहते-सौदा करते थे।

panels.shot: tight close-up on overlapping manuscripts in different scripts
panels.action: Italian Latin, elegant Persian script, and Portuguese lines lie overlapping on a table, each page naming Vijayanagara with varied spellings, numbers and metaphors.

dialogue.text: Yet their texts are not simple mirrors. Razzaq’s ornate style, Paes’s trader’s eye, Conti’s memory, Nunes’s compilations—each bends the city slightly to his own purpose and language.

i18n.hi: फिर भी उनके लेख सीधे आईने नहीं — रज़्ज़ाक की नफ़ीस ज़बान, पायस की सौदागरी नज़र, कॉन्ती की यादें, नूनेश की जोड़-तोड़ — हर कोई शहर को अपनी मर्ज़ी और भाषा से थोड़ा मोड़ देता है।

panels.shot: medium shot of a modern historian reading these accounts
panels.action: A modern historian in a quiet library studies critical editions of Conti, Razzaq, Paes and Nunes, margin notes and maps spread around, as they weigh numbers and claims.

dialogue.text: Recent scholars, like those of Textures of Time, warn us: count their elephants and soldiers with care, listen to their wonder, but test every number against archaeology, inscriptions and other eyes.

i18n.hi: आज के इतिहासकार, जैसे ‘टेक्स्चर्स ऑफ टाइम’ वाले, टोकते हैं — उनके हाथी और सिपाहियों को सोच-समझकर गिनो, उनकी हैरत सुनो, पर हर आंकड़ा को खुदाई, शिलालेख और दूसरी नज़रों से परखो।

panels.shot: final wide shot of ruined Hampi with ghostly figures of the four travelers
panels.action: The Four Witnesses of Vijayanagara appear faded above the ruined stone pavilions and surviving temple towers of Hampi at sunset, the Tungabhadra river glinting in the distance.

dialogue.text: Their words still guide us through Vijayanagara’s vanished streets—reminding us to cherish eyewitnesses for their vividness, but to walk beside them with questions, not blind belief.

i18n.hi: उनके शब्द हमें अब भी विजयनगर की मिट चुकी गलियों से गुज़ारते हैं — गवाहों की चमक को सँभालते हुए भी याद दिलाते हैं कि उनके साथ चलो, मगर सवालों के साथ, अंधी यक़ीन से नहीं।

subtitle_i18n.en: Four Witnesses to the City of Victory
